title: Gravy on Cotton
stainChemistry: Gravy is four components layered together: meat protein (from stock or drippings), animal fat (triglyceride), starch (flour or cornstarch thickener) that forms a matrix holding the other three against the fiber, and sometimes a tannin or caramel component from pan browning. Cotton is cellulose — tolerates enzymes, NSD, and 3% H₂O₂ at room temperature. The constraints are ordering-based, not fiber-based: starch must be broken down mechanically or with amylase before water, and protein must be cleared by enzyme before any heat, or the whole stain matrix cooks into the fabric.
whyThisWorks: Starch first, mechanically — scrape the dried film off. Then enzyme (protease digests meat protein, amylase handles residual starch; most modern enzyme pretreats include both). NSD emulsifies the fat the protein matrix was holding in place. 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes any residual brown pigment from pan-browning. Hot wash only after the protein is gone — safe at that point because there's nothing left for heat to set.
defaultAssumption: Fresh gravy is an easy clean; dried gravy (hours+) is harder because the starch matrix has locked the other components in a brittle film that has to be cracked open before chemistry can act. Heat-set (already ironed or hot-dried) escalates — the protein has cooked into the fiber and home recovery is unreliable.

spottingProtocol.technique: Remove starch bulk, then cold flush
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade or spoon edge, cold water, white terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape dried gravy off with a dull blade — don't rub, scraping lifts the starch film cleanly while rubbing drives it into the weave. Then flush cold water from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — warm water sets the meat protein into cotton.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease + amylase pretreat) in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply the Protein Spotter or dissolve a multi-enzyme pretreat in warm water (warm, not hot — enzymes denature above 50°C). Submerge the stain or keep it wet under a damp towel. Brush gently with a soft bristle brush after the first 10 minutes. Protease breaks the meat protein; amylase, if present in the formulation, digests any residual starch the scrape missed.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. NSD emulsifies the animal fat the enzyme freed from the matrix. Rinse cool from the back.

For residual brown shadow from pan-browning or caramelization: apply 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Peroxide oxidizes the browning compounds to colorless. One oxidizer at a time; NSD-flush before a second pass.
Rinse until the water runs clear. Launder hot per the care label. Safe at this point — the protein has been digested, so heat is on the finishing side, not the setting side.

steps: 1. Scrape the dried film off. Cold flush from the back.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter (protease + amylase) in warm water (30–38°C). Dwell 20–30 min.
3. NSD to emulsify the fat. Light brush.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual browning. One pass. NSD-flush between any repeat.
5. Cool rinse + hot wash per care label.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease/amylase pretreat)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush; warm (30–38°C) for enzyme; cool through NSD + peroxide; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never warm-flush fresh gravy — sets the meat protein
Enzyme window is warm, not hot — above 50°C the protease denatures
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between

homeSolutions.instruction: Get the solid gravy off the fabric before any water. Use a dull butter knife or the edge of a credit card. If it's dried, it'll pop off in brittle flakes — that's the starch film. Don't rub; scraping lifts the film, rubbing grinds it into the weave.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Flush From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water for a minute. Let the water push the gravy out the way it came in. Never use warm or hot water yet — the meat protein in gravy cooks into cotton if you do.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Warm Water, Not Hot
homeSolutions.instruction: Use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout — these have both protein- and starch-digesting enzymes). Dissolve in WARM water per the label. Apply to the stain, let it sit 20–30 minutes. The enzymes dismantle the meat protein and any residual starch.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush for the Fat
homeSolutions.instruction: Add a drop of Dawn dish soap and work it in gently with a soft toothbrush or fingertip. Dawn emulsifies the animal fat the enzyme freed. Rinse cool.
If a brown shadow remains from pan-browning: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot. Let it bubble for 3–5 minutes, blot. Skip this step on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse cool from the back until the towel runs clean. Then launder on the hottest setting the care label allows — safe now that the protein is digested. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Any residual stain cures permanently under dryer heat.

If the gravy's been ironed or hot-dried, or it survives two full home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Gravy on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], treated at home with enzyme pretreat + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide. No heat until after enzyme."

steps: 1. Scrape the dried film off with a dull blade. Don't rub.
2. Cold water flush from the BACK of the fabric.
3. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Zout) dissolved in WARM water. Dwell 20–30 min.
4. Dawn dish soap + soft brush to lift the fat.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual brown shadow. Skip on dark cotton.
7. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if gravy has been through a hot dryer or iron, two home rounds haven't cleared it, or the cotton is dark dyed and peroxide isn't safe. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: Never warm-flush fresh gravy — sets the meat protein
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm water for the pretreat, not hot
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton or inside-seam-test first

neverDo: Never apply hot water before the enzyme step — cooks the meat protein in
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time, flushed between
Never rub wet gravy into the fiber — scrape first
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot wash after chemistry
Cotton is heat-durable once protein is gone

materialWarnings: Protein first, heat after — reverse and the stain sets
Scrape before water — rubbing drives starch deeper
Peroxide on whites; inside-seam-test dark cotton

escalation.when: Gravy already ironed or hot-dried, two home rounds failed, or dark/dyed cotton where peroxide isn't safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Gravy on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, older?
Has it been ironed or hot-dried since?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
Heavy gravy (dark pan-browning) or light gravy (pale roux)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Protein-before-heat is the one hard rule. Dried starch film requires mechanical scraping before chemistry can reach the rest of the stain. Dark pan-browning increases peroxide passes; light gravy often clears without peroxide at all.
